title: Matching Terahertz and Hall Mobilities as a Hallmark of Intrinsic Charge Transport in Metal-Halide Perovskites
authors: Dmitry R. Maslennikov, Ben P. Carwithen, Vladimir V. Bruevich, Yichao Cai, Davide Nodari, Navendu Mondal, Xijia Zheng, Beier Hu, Nicola Gasparini, Jarvist M. Frost, Vitaly Podzorov, Artem A. Bakulin
abstract: Charge-carrier transport in soft-lattice materials, including metal-halide perovskites, is often perceived to be highly heterogeneous across different length scales, and influenced by both the intrinsic (dynamic) thermal electronic disorder and extrinsic (static) disorder due to crystal defects, impurities, grain boundaries, and surface states. As a consequence, the reported carrier mobilities obtained by different electrical and optical measurement techniques frequently disagree, raising a critical question: can a truly intrinsic charge transport regime (that is, a regime not dominated by static disorder) extend across macroscopic single crystals of these materials? Here, we demonstrate such a regime in an exemplary metal-halide perovskite system, epitaxial CsPbBr$_{3}$ single crystals, where the local mobility obtained via optical pump-terahertz probe (OPTP) spectroscopy quantitatively agrees with the macroscopic transport mobility across a broad range of experimental conditions. Using a dedicated device platform that enables concurrent Hall-effect and OPTP measurements on the same single-crystalline sample, we obtain consistent room-temperature mobilities of ~ 30 cm$^{2}$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, among the highest reliably reported for CsPbBr$_{3}$. Both techniques reveal band-like temperature dependence of the hole mobility with similar power exponents, confirming that the same intrinsic transport mechanism governs the ultrafast/local and steady-state/macroscopic responses. These results show that defect-free charge transport is achievable in soft-lattice perovskites on millimetre length scales and establish a robust methodology for benchmarking intrinsic mobility in emerging semiconductors.
summary: This work addresses discrepancies in measuring carrier mobility in perovskites by simultaneously employing Hall effect and OPTP spectroscopy on single crystals. By showing quantitative agreement between local (OPTP) and macroscopic (Hall) mobilities, the authors establish a robust benchmark for intrinsic charge transport in soft-lattice semiconductors.
main_problem: To resolve discrepancies in reported charge carrier mobilities in soft-lattice metal-halide perovskites, which are attributed to heterogeneity across length scales and static disorder.
main_result: The study demonstrated quantitative agreement between local mobility measured by OPTP spectroscopy and macroscopic mobility measured by the Hall effect on single-crystal $	ext{CsPbBr}_3$. This confirms that intrinsic charge transport is achievable in these soft materials.
method: A novel, integrated device platform was used to perform concurrent Hall-effect (macroscopic) and Optical Pump–Terahertz Probe (OPTP) spectroscopy (local/ultrafast) measurements on the same single crystal.
model_or_system: The physical system is epitaxial $	ext{CsPbBr}_3$ single crystals. The analysis compares steady-state transport ($\mu_{	ext{Hall}}$) with ultrafast, photoexcited dynamics ($\mu_{	ext{OPTP}}$), linking local electronic response to bulk electrical properties.
key_observables: Charge carrier mobility ($\mu$), temperature dependence of mobility ($\mu(T)$), and the comparison between $\mu_{	ext{OPTP}}$ and $\mu_{	ext{Hall}}$.
important_parameters: Room-temperature mobilities ($\sim 30 	ext{ cm}^2	ext{V}^{-1}	ext{s}^{-1}$); temperature dependence exponents ($b \approx 1.1-1.3$); excitation fluence thresholds (e.g., $40 \, \mu	ext{J}/	ext{cm}^2$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the agreement between $\mu_{	ext{OPTP}}$ and $\mu_{	ext{Hall}}$ signifies a true intrinsic transport regime, free from static disorder limitations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device schematics for co-measurement, comparisons of mobility values derived from both techniques across different conditions (temperature/fluence), and plots showing temperature dependence ($\mu(T) \propto T^{-b}$) and fluence-dependent OPTP transients.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of measurement discrepancy, details the novel dual-measurement platform on single crystals, presents quantitative agreement between local and macroscopic mobilities, analyzes the common temperature scaling laws, and discusses high-fluence regimes where many-body effects become dominant.
why_interesting: While focused on solid-state physics, the rigorous methodology of linking ultrafast optical measurements to steady-state transport is highly relevant for understanding charge dynamics in complex quantum materials, which often exhibit non-equilibrium behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Optical microscopy image of the studied CsPbBr3 device with graphite contacts and parylene-N capping layer that allows both the (dark) Hall effect and THz OPTP measurements of the same device. (b) A sketch of the device structure and an overview of the Hall effect and OPTP measurements. The approach allows both electrical transport and transmission spectroscopy measurements in a single device. (c) An example of the Hall voltage measurements of the device shown in panels a and b carried out at 300 K in the dark, revealing an excellent signal-to-noise ratio: the transport (Hall) mobility is extracted from the modulations of the Hall voltage induced by the external magnetic...

Figure 2. Temperature-dependent measurements of the carrier mobility in epitaxial single-crystal CsPbBr3 perovskite films. (a) The mobility extracted from OPTP and Hall measurements in the ranges 125 – 300 K and 200 – 315 K, respectively. The μ(T) follows a power-law dependence, 𝜇(𝑇) ∝ 𝑇−b , with the power exponent indicated on the plot (Pearson’s correlation coefficient R = 0.99 in both cases). (b) The OPTP transients corresponding to the extracted 𝜇𝑂𝑃𝑇𝑃(𝑇) recorded at a fluence of ≈ 8.4 W/cm2 (excitation pulse power ≈ 100 W) and subsequently fitted by a sum of two exponential functions convolved with a Gaussian.

Figure 3. (a, b) Fluence dependence of the OPTP transients in epitaxial single- crystalline CsPbBr3 films under a 400 nm excitation recorded at 300 and 93 K. (c) The OPTP signal at 300 ps after photoexcitation as a function of excitation fluence for these temperatures. The kink observed on the curves at approximately 9 and 40 μJ/cm2 signal a change in the charge carrier dynamics and recombination regime. (d) Fluence dependant PL spectra of the same sample under the same photoexcitation (400 nm, 35 fs laser pulses).
